title: Indications, Discontinuities, and Defects Defined
narrationScript: Every radiograph you read ends in a decision, and that decision rides on three words that sound interchangeable but are not. Indication. Discontinuity. Defect. Use them loosely and you will reject good parts, accept bad ones, and write reports that fall apart when an auditor reads them. Use them precisely and your reports say exactly what you found, what it was, and what the code says about it. Let us lock the three words down. An indication is anything you see. More exactly, it is any localized variation in film density that demands an explanation. The moment you notice a dark spot, a light spot, a line, or a band on the radiograph, you have an indication, and that is all you have. You do not yet know whether it came from the part, the film, or the technique. Calling everything you see an indication is not being timid. It is being accurate, because the next step is interpretation, and interpretation is where you decide what produced it. Interpretation sorts every indication into one of three bins. A false indication was not produced by the part at all. It came from the film and its handling. Crescent shaped crimp marks from sharp bending, dark pressure marks, branching static exposures, streaks and spots from bad processing, fog from light leaks or age. The condition lives on the film, not in the steel. A classic test is to compare two films exposed together in the same cassette. A condition in the part prints in the same place on both films. A film artifact shows on one film only, and it will not repeat in the same spot if you reshoot. A nonrelevant indication is the opposite case. It was produced by the part, but by a real feature that is not a discontinuity. The image of a backing ring. The edge of an adjacent member. Weld spatter sitting on the surface. Grinding marks, or the ripple of the weld crown. These are real, but they are geometry and surface condition, and many of them can be cleared by simply looking at the part. A relevant indication is the one that matters. It was produced by a discontinuity, and it moves forward to evaluation. So what is a discontinuity? It is any interruption in the normal structure of the part. A void, an inclusion, a plane where fusion never happened, a crack where the metal tore. Notice the definition says nothing about good or bad. A discontinuity is a fact, not a verdict. Almost every weld and casting ever made contains some discontinuity at some scale, and most of them never matter. Whether one matters is not your opinion and not your gut feel. It is a comparison against acceptance criteria, and that comparison is called evaluation. The applicable code, standard, or specification states what is acceptable by type, size, location, and concentration. A typical piping rule might accept isolated rounded porosity up to a stated fraction of the wall thickness, limit an individual slag inclusion to roughly one third of the wall, and reject any crack or incomplete fusion outright, no matter how short, because sharp planar flaws concentrate stress out of all proportion to their volume. When a discontinuity passes those limits, it stays a discontinuity and the part ships with it. When it exceeds them, and only then, it becomes a defect. A defect is a discontinuity that fails the acceptance criteria, which makes the part rejectable until it is repaired or formally dispositioned. The word defect means reject. Every defect is a discontinuity, but only a fraction of discontinuities are ever defects, and the same discontinuity can be acceptable on one job and rejectable on the next, because the criteria belong to the contract, not to the flaw. One more wrinkle. Parts do not stop developing discontinuities when they leave the shop. Service creates its own. Fatigue cracks grow from thread roots and tool marks under cyclic load. Corrosion thins walls and digs pits, including hidden attack under insulation. Erosion scours elbows. High temperature breeds creep damage, and hydrogen and stress corrosion do their own quiet work. An in service radiograph is read with the same three word logic, but often against fitness for service criteria rather than new construction criteria, and a finding may be measured and monitored instead of repaired. Same discipline, different rule book. Keep the words straight, name the rule book on your report, and your call will stand up to anyone who reads it.
imagePrompt: A film interpreter in a darkened reading room leans over a high-intensity illuminator studying a pipe weld radiograph, one hand resting on a densitometer, with reference radiographs and a closed code binder stacked beside the viewer.

sections.heading: Three Words, Three Different Claims
sections.body:
In radiography the words **indication**, **discontinuity**, and **defect** are not synonyms - they are three rungs of a ladder, and each rung makes a stronger claim than the last. An **indication** is anything on the radiograph that demands an explanation: a localized change in film density - darker or lighter than its surroundings - that you have not yet accounted for. At the moment you spot it, that is all it is. You do not yet know whether it came from the part, from the film, or from the technique, so the only honest word for it is indication.

The discipline matters because your report is a technical and contractual document. Write "defect" next to a harmless film mark and you have rejected a sound weld on paper. Write "indication" where the code demanded an evaluation and you have shipped a decision you never actually made. Auditors, customers, and - in the worst cases - failure investigators read these words literally. A Level II who keeps the ladder straight produces reports that mean exactly what they say, and that precision is the foundation everything else in this lesson stands on.

sections.caption: The road from indication to disposition: interpretation sorts indications into false, nonrelevant, and relevant; evaluation against acceptance criteria decides whether a discontinuity is acceptable or a defect.

sections.heading: Interpretation: False, Nonrelevant, Relevant
sections.body:
**Interpretation** is the step that explains the indication - deciding what produced it. Every indication lands in one of three bins.

- **False indications** are not produced by the part at all; they live on the film. Crescent-shaped crimp marks from kinking the film, dark pressure marks, lightning-like static discharges, chemical streaks and spotting from sloppy processing, fog from age, heat, or a light leak. The fastest confirmation: when two films were exposed together in the same cassette, a film artifact appears on one film only, while a condition in the part prints on both in the same place. Failing that, reshoot - artifacts do not repeat in the same spot.
- **Nonrelevant indications** are produced by the part, but by a real feature that is not a discontinuity: the uniform image of a backing ring, the edge of an adjacent member, weld spatter, surface grinding marks, the ripple of an as-welded crown. Many are cleared by simply examining the surface - if you can see and touch the cause, record what it is.
- **Relevant indications** are produced by a discontinuity. Only these move forward to evaluation.

Interpretation is a skill of elimination: rule out the film, rule out geometry and surface condition, and what remains is a discontinuity that must be named and measured.

sections.caption: One simulated film, three different calls: a crimp-mark artifact (false), surface spatter verified on the part (nonrelevant), and a slag line that must be evaluated (relevant).

sections.heading: Discontinuity: A Fact, Not a Verdict
sections.body:
A **discontinuity** is an interruption in the normal physical structure of a part - a void where metal should be, an inclusion of foreign material, a plane where fusion never happened, a crack where the metal tore. The definition is deliberately neutral. It says nothing about whether the part is good or bad, and it carries no judgment about size, cause, or consequence.

Two points follow. First, discontinuities are normal. Real castings shrink as they solidify, real welds trap an occasional gas bubble, and rolled plate carries flattened remnants of the ingot it came from. At some scale virtually every industrial part contains discontinuities, and the overwhelming majority never threaten service. Second, "discontinuity" is a statement of fact you can defend from the film alone: *there is an elongated slag inclusion two inches long at the fusion zone* is observation. Whether it is **acceptable** is a different question answered by a different authority. Keeping fact separate from verdict is what lets two qualified readers at two different companies agree on what a film shows, even when their codes disagree about what to do with it.

sections.title: The ladder in one sentence
sections.body: Every **defect** is a discontinuity, and every discontinuity that shows on film produces an indication - but the reverse is never automatic. *Indication* means "something to explain." *Discontinuity* means "a real interruption in the part." **Defect** means "a discontinuity that fails the applicable acceptance criteria - rejectable." Never write the word defect on a report until evaluation against the governing criteria is complete.

sections.heading: Evaluation and Acceptance Criteria
sections.body:
**Evaluation** is the comparison of a known discontinuity against **acceptance criteria** - the limits stated in the code, standard, or specification governing the job. Criteria typically control four things: the **type** of discontinuity, its **size**, its **location**, and its **concentration**. The patterns repeat across fabrication codes: rounded porosity is commonly judged against charts scaled to wall thickness; an individual elongated slag inclusion is often limited to a stated fraction of the wall - on the order of one-third of the thickness in many piping rules - with a further cap on total accumulated length in any given length of weld; and cracks, incomplete fusion, and (in most full-penetration work) incomplete penetration are rejected outright at any size, because sharp planar discontinuities concentrate stress out of all proportion to their volume.

When the discontinuity is within limits, it remains a discontinuity and the part is accepted with it. When it exceeds them - and only then - it becomes a **defect**: rejectable, to be repaired, removed, or formally dispositioned by engineering. Defect status is granted by the criteria, not by the discontinuity itself. The same two-inch slag line can pass under one code and fail under another, so your report must always identify the criteria you evaluated against - code, edition, and paragraph - or the words accept and reject mean nothing.

sections.heading: Service-Induced Discontinuities
sections.body:
The three-word ladder follows a part through its whole life, because parts keep developing discontinuities after fabrication. **Service-induced discontinuities** originate in operation: **fatigue cracks** that start at stress concentrations - thread roots, sharp fillets, tool marks, weld toes - and grow with each load cycle; **corrosion** that thins walls and digs isolated pits, including hidden attack under insulation; **erosion** that scours elbows and anything downstream of a flow disturbance; **creep** damage in high-temperature service; **hydrogen damage** and **stress-corrosion cracking** in the environments that drive them.

Radiography earns its keep here: profile and tangential shots image remaining wall through insulation on operating lines, and pit depth can be estimated by comparing film density through the pit against the surrounding full wall. But the rule book changes. In-service findings are commonly judged by fitness-for-service methods rather than the original construction code, and a flaw may be measured, monitored, and left in place under engineering evaluation rather than repaired. A discontinuity that was acceptable on the construction radiographs may be found grown at an outage - which is precisely why those original films, with their documented interpretations, are retained as the baseline against which service degradation is measured.

sections.caption: Sorting the language: what you see, what it is, and what you call it.
rows: Crescent-shaped dark mark; absent on the companion film from the same cassette
Film crimp artifact - it lives on the film, not in the part
Uniform light band matching the backing ring you can verify on the assembly
Real feature of the part or assembly, but not a discontinuity
Rounded dark spots within the code's porosity limits
A real discontinuity that passes the acceptance criteria - the part ships with it
Fine, wandering dark line in the weld image
Cracks exceed acceptance criteria in virtually every fabrication code, at any length
Dark band at the weld toe deeper than the stated undercut allowance
Exceeds the governing limit - rejectable until repaired or dispositioned

figures.title: From indication to disposition: the evaluation decision flow
One film, three calls: false, nonrelevant, and relevant indications

checkQuestions.question: Reading a radiograph, you spot a localized dark density change you cannot yet explain. The correct term for it at this moment is:
options: A defect, since it is clearly visible
A discontinuity, since something must have caused it
An indication, until interpretation establishes what produced it
A rejectable condition pending repair
checkQuestions.explanation: Indication is the neutral term for any density change requiring explanation; interpretation then classifies it as false, nonrelevant, or relevant before any stronger word is justified.

checkQuestions.question: Two films were loaded in the same cassette for one exposure. A crescent-shaped dark mark appears on one film but not the other. This is best classified as:
options: A relevant indication - evaluate it against the code
A false indication - a film-handling artifact such as a crimp mark
A nonrelevant indication caused by part geometry
Incomplete penetration at the weld root
checkQuestions.explanation: A condition in the part prints in the same place on both films of a double-film load; a mark on only one film lives on that film - here a classic crescent crimp mark from kinking.

checkQuestions.question: A straight, uniform light band parallel to the weld matches the backing ring you can verify on the assembly. The indication is:
options: False, because it is not a discontinuity
Nonrelevant - produced by a real part feature that is not a discontinuity
Relevant - backing rings must be evaluated as inclusions
A defect unless the backing ring is removed
checkQuestions.explanation: Nonrelevant indications come from real features of the part or assembly - backing rings, geometry, spatter - confirmed by examining the object; false indications come from the film itself.

checkQuestions.question: What, precisely, turns a discontinuity into a defect?
options: Being visible on the radiograph at normal viewing distance
Being larger than one-quarter inch in any direction
Failing the acceptance criteria of the governing code or specification
The interpreter's professional judgment that it looks harmful
checkQuestions.explanation: Defect is a criteria-defined status: a discontinuity whose type, size, location, or concentration exceeds the applicable acceptance criteria, making it rejectable. The criteria - not appearance or gut feel - grant that status.

checkQuestions.question: Which of the following is a service-induced discontinuity?
options: A cold shut in a pump casing casting
An elongated slag inclusion in a weld
A fatigue crack growing from a thread root after years of cyclic loading
A lamination in rolled plate
checkQuestions.explanation: Fatigue cracks initiate and grow in operation under cyclic load. Cold shuts are inherent solidification discontinuities, while slag inclusions and laminations originate in processing.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course 3.1-3.3
Standard NDT terminology definitions for indication, discontinuity, and defect (ASTM E1316 style, general reference)
Fabrication-code acceptance criteria patterns (ASME Section V with referencing construction codes, B31-style piping rules - general reference)
Fitness-for-service evaluation concepts for in-service findings (API 579 / ASME FFS style, general reference)
SNT-TC-1A Level II responsibilities: interpretation and evaluation (general reference)
